did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, specifically ResNet-50 with transfer learning, to perform skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset. The study employed data augmentation and up-sampling techniques to handle class imbalance and achieved a 98% accuracy rate in classifying skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of a ResNet-50 CNN model with up-sampling and augmentation techniques on the HAM10000 dataset to address class imbalance in skin cancer classification. It reports specific new results, including a 98% accuracy rate, precision, recall, and F1-scores, and details the methodology, architecture, and training process, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Employing Up-sampling and Augmentation for Imbalanced Datasets in Skin Cancer Classification using CNN Architecture
journal: 2024 8th International Conference on Information Technology, Information Systems and Electrical Engineering (ICITISEE)
brief_summary: This study addresses class imbalance in skin cancer classification by employing up-sampling and data augmentation techniques on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset. The researchers utilized a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network with transfer learning to classify seven distinct skin lesion categories. To mitigate imbalance, the original 10,015 images were expanded to 45,844 through up-sampling and eight specific augmentation methods, including rotation, shifting, and zooming. The model was evaluated using precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC metrics, achieving an overall accuracy of 98%. The results demonstrate that this approach significantly improves classification performance compared to previous studies, highlighting the effectiveness of handling data imbalance for robust skin lesion diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven distinct categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. It had the largest number of samples in the original imbalanced dataset.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion categories (nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Class 0 to Class 6) using a ResNet-50 architecture with a softmax output layer. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (98%), precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, originally containing 10,015 images. The dataset was subjected to up-sampling and data augmentation to address class imbalance, resulting in a final dataset of 45,844 images.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet-50 model after up-sampling and augmentation. The dataset was split into 80% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation during training (loss and accuracy trends were monitored), but specific count is not reported.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's classification performance. The dataset was split into 20% for testing.
use.other: The original imbalanced distribution (nv: 6705, mel: 1113, bkl: 1099, bcc: 514, akiec: 327, vasc: 142, df: 115) was used to demonstrate the imbalance problem before up-sampling.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize performance metrics for each category and overall averages, identifying specific classification errors (e.g., 72 incorrect predictions for 'bkl' class, 3 for 'vasc' class).
To evaluate the classification performance of the model for each of the seven skin lesion classes and overall accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between classes by illustrating the relationship between True Positive Rate (TPR) and False Positive Rate (FPR) at various thresholds; AUC values were reported to be between 0.99 and 1.00.
To measure the overall proportion of correct predictions, achieving 98% on the test set.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

HAM10000.dataset_description: The HAM10000 dataset was used, consisting of 10,015 original images. After up-sampling and augmentation, the dataset expanded to 45,844 images with balanced class distributions.

experiment.model: ResNet-50 with transfer learning (pre-trained weights from ImageNet, frozen parameters in specific layers)
preprocessing: Data augmentation (Rescale 1./255, Rotation 10, Width Shift 0.1, Height Shift 0.1, Zoom 0.1, Flipping horizontal and vertical, Fill mode nearest)

bkl.notes: Most classification errors occurred in this class (72 incorrect predictions out of 1,397 total predictions for this class)

vasc.notes: Best classification performance observed; only three prediction errors

experiment.notes: The model achieved optimal performance without overfitting. The training and validation loss trends converged to stable values. The proposed method's accuracy of 98% surpassed previous studies.

architecture_details.backbone: ResNet-50 convolutional neural network with skip connections
custom_layers: Dense layer with 128 units and ReLU activation
Dense layer with 64 units and ReLU activation
architecture_details.frozen_layers: Specific layers in ResNet-50 were frozen to prevent weight updates during training

preprocessing_techniques: Up-sampling to balance class distribution (expanding dataset from 10,015 to 45,844 images)
Data augmentation including: Rescaling (1./255), Rotation (10 degrees), Width Shift (0.1), Height Shift (0.1), Zoom (0.1), Horizontal and Vertical Flipping, Fill mode nearest

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rescaled pixel values by 1./255 to ensure consistent input data.
Applied horizontal and vertical flipping to create mirrored images.
Used 'nearest' fill mode to maintain image integrity by filling empty spaces with nearby pixel values.

conclusions: The proposed method using ResNet-50 with up-sampling and data augmentation achieved an overall accuracy of 98% on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset, surpassing previous studies (e.g., Hoang et al. at 86.33%, Garg et al. at 90.51%, and Shetty et al. at 95.18%).

Up-sampling and data augmentation effectively addressed class imbalance by expanding the dataset from 10,015 to 45,844 images with balanced class distributions, which improved model generalization and reduced bias towards majority classes.
The model demonstrated excellent performance in distinguishing between classes, with ROC-AUC values ranging from 0.99 to 1.00, indicating high reliability in classification tasks.

Specific classes such as 'vasc' (Vascular lesions), 'df' (Dermatofibroma), 'bcc' (Basal cell carcinoma), and 'akiec' (Actinic keratosis) achieved 100% precision, recall, and F1-scores, while 'nv' (Melanocytic nevi) had the lowest recall (90%) and F1-score (93%).
The most classification errors occurred in the 'bkl' (Benign keratosis) class, with 72 incorrect predictions, likely due to higher intra-class variation and similarity to other classes.
The training process showed optimal performance without overfitting, as evidenced by converging training and validation loss trends to stable values after 22 epochs.

Future research should focus on refining preprocessing techniques, such as advancing augmentation methods (e.g., SMOTE), implementing class weighting strategies, and reducing image noise to further enhance diagnostic accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance and varying ground truth verification methods (histopathology, follow-up, consensus, confocal microscopy), potentially introducing label noise and bias.

The dataset was artificially balanced using up-sampling (expanding from 10,015 to 45,844 images) and data augmentation; this synthetic balancing may lead to overfitting or inflated performance metrics that do not generalize to real-world imbalanced clinical distributions.

The paper does not report an independent external validation set; the 20% test split is drawn from the same augmented dataset, raising concerns about data leakage and over-optimistic performance estimates.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the proposed method's 98% accuracy against baseline studies, making claims of 'significant superiority' statistically unsupported.

The model achieved 100% precision, recall, and F1-score for classes like 'vasc' and 'df', which may indicate potential data leakage or insufficient difficulty in distinguishing these classes, warranting scrutiny.
The most classification errors occurred in the 'bkl' (Benign keratosis) class (72 incorrect predictions), highlighting persistent challenges in distinguishing this class despite augmentation efforts.

The study is a conference paper with limited scope; it does not address deployment concerns, computational efficiency in real-time settings, or robustness to image noise and resolution variations found in clinical practice.

The authors acknowledge that future work should explore advanced augmentation (e.g., SMOTE) and class weighting, implying the current method may not be the optimal solution for handling severe class imbalance.
